behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is the extractor missing a key term — this is a quality-of-extraction failure, not a module-wiring failure. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network/subprocess dependency. LLM-DEP: 'Identifies all key terms' is a claim about AI output quality. A stub extractor always returns whatever we hardcode; it cannot reveal that the real model misses an indirect obligation phrased as a conditional or a penalty buried in a definitions section. Only a real LLM call can fail in the way the behavior describes. REJECTION-GATE: There is no text in the behavior disqualifying Agentic — nothing says 'routing' or 'retry' or any structural concern that a stub could exercise. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: EVIDENCE-LEVEL = Agentic; OUTPUT-LEVEL = Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real legal contract containing at least one party name, one date, one obligation, and one penalty clause — including at least one term expressed indirectly (e.g., obligation as a conditional 'shall be required to') — to the real clause extractor. Assert that the returned extraction contains all four category types and that the indirect term is present. Run against the real LLM to expose extraction gaps that stubs would always hide.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is a real model failing to recognize semantic equivalence between euphemistic phrasing and prohibited language — this is not a module-wiring problem. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network dependency. LLM-DEP: 'Indirect or euphemistic' phrasing is precisely the class of input where stub responses are useless. A stub always returns whatever we program; only a real LLM can fail to connect 'termination for convenience at the sole discretion of one party' to a high-risk clause. MOCK-HIDE: Any mock that passes here is a tautology — we would be asserting against our own fabricated output. REJECTION-GATE: Nothing in the behavior text limits the concern to routing, retry, or structural policy application; it is entirely about semantic comprehension quality. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: EVIDENCE-LEVEL = Agentic; OUTPUT-LEVEL = Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Provide the real risk assessor with a contract clause that expresses a high-risk condition through indirect or euphemistic language (e.g., 'the party reserves the right to adjust compensation at its discretion' instead of 'unilateral pay reduction'). Configure the risk policy to flag unilateral compensation changes. Assert that the assessor returns a high-risk flag for that clause. Must use the real LLM — a stub would trivially pass by returning a hardcoded flag.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is the model misapplying or missing a regulatory requirement for the jurisdiction — a quality-of-determination failure, not a structural one. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network/subprocess dependency. LLM-DEP: 'Correctly determines' compliance against real regulatory requirements is an AI judgment task. A stub returning a fixed compliance result cannot expose that the real model applies the wrong jurisdiction's rules or misses a required disclosure clause. MOCK-HIDE: Stubbing always passes, completely masking the failure. REJECTION-GATE: No behavior text limits the concern to routing or configuration loading; the entire claim is about the correctness of the AI determination. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: EVIDENCE-LEVEL = Agentic; OUTPUT-LEVEL = Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the compliance checker for a specific jurisdiction (e.g., GDPR for EU contracts). Submit a contract that has one clearly compliant clause and one clause that violates a known requirement for that jurisdiction (e.g., missing data retention limit). Assert that the real compliance checker returns compliant for the first clause and non-compliant with the correct regulatory citation for the second. Must run against the real LLM.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is in the merge function's conflict-handling logic — specifically whether it drops one assessment or loses attribution when two ReviewerOutput objects disagree on the same clause ID. This is a deterministic algorithmic failure fully exercisable with constructed inputs. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network. LLM-DEP: The merge step logic is described as combining outputs — there is no claim it calls an LLM to resolve conflicts; it 'preserves both assessments with attribution', which is a structural preservation guarantee. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network needed. (b) Merge correctness does not depend on real AI output — we supply fixed conflicting inputs. (c) No chaining of agentic steps. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior explicitly describes a deterministic invariant ('preserves both assessments with attribution') fully testable with two hardcoded conflicting ReviewerOutput objects passed directly to the merge function. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: EVIDENCE-LEVEL = Unit; OUTPUT-LEVEL = Unit.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two ReviewerOutput objects for the same clause ID: one from the extractor with assessment='standard' and one from the risk assessor with assessment='high_risk'. Pass both directly to the merge function. Assert that the merged output for that clause contains two entries, each retaining its original assessment value and reviewer attribution. No LLM calls or multi-module wiring required.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is the orchestration losing findings — e.g., a parallel dispatch race condition drops one reviewer's result, or the merge step receives incomplete input because collection failed silently. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network required in principle. LLM-DEP: 'For a real contract' implies real reviewer execution. More importantly: MULTI-STEP: this behavior explicitly chains three independent agentic steps (clause extractor, risk assessor, compliance checker) in parallel followed by a merge step. That is the definition of a multi-step agentic workflow. A stub for any one reviewer would hide whether the real pipeline correctly waits for all three real LLM calls and passes all three result sets — without gaps — into merge. REJECTION-GATE: No text limits this to orchestration-only concerns that stubs could exercise; the behavior explicitly requires all three reviewers to run and all findings to survive to the final report. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: EVIDENCE-LEVEL = Workflow; OUTPUT-LEVEL = Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real contract to the full pipeline end-to-end using all three real AI reviewers. After the unified report is produced, assert: (1) the report contains at least one finding attributed to each of the three reviewers, (2) no reviewer's output count in the unified report is zero, and (3) any clause that appeared in multiple reviewer outputs appears with all corresponding assessments. Run the pipeline twice to check for non-deterministic loss due to parallel dispatch timing.
